Version
  • Theory
  • Solar Radiation Model
  • Solar Radiation Model

Solar Radiation Model

The solar model is a Julia port of the Greenhouses Modelica Library Solar_model.mo (Altes-Buch 2019, after de Zwart 1996 and Vanthoor 2011). It splits the global horizontal irradiance $I_{glob}$ [W/m²] into PAR + NIR, applies cover transmission, distributes the transmitted radiation across canopy and floor using a multi-layer reflection model, and accounts separately for the radiation released to the air via construction obstacles.

Spectral split

\[I_{PAR} = \eta_{PAR} \cdot I_{glob}, \quad I_{NIR} = \eta_{NIR} \cdot I_{glob}\]

with $\eta_{PAR} = \eta_{NIR} = 0.5$ (equal energy split — Vanthoor 2011 default).

Cover absorption

The cover absorbs a small fraction of incident radiation:

\[R_{sun \to cov} = (\alpha_{cov,PAR} \cdot \eta_{PAR} + \alpha_{cov,NIR} \cdot \eta_{NIR}) \cdot I_{glob}\]

ParameterPARNIRSource
$\tau_{cov}$0.890.87Single-pane glass
$\rho_{cov}$0.080.10Single-pane glass
$\alpha_{cov} = 1 - \tau - \rho$0.030.03derived

Glass is nearly transparent to shortwave; ~3 % of the total is absorbed at each pass (more at multi-bounce, see below).

Multi-layer NIR distribution (canopy + floor + cover)

Unlike a simple Beer-Lambert intercept, NIR is partly reflected by the floor and the canopy. These reflected components bounce between the cover, the canopy, and the floor before being absorbed somewhere. The multilayer_tau_rho helper composes two semi-transparent layers analytically:

\[\tau_{12} = \frac{\tau_1 \tau_2}{1 - \rho_1 \rho_2}, \qquad \rho_{12} = \rho_1 + \frac{\tau_1^2 \rho_2}{1 - \rho_1 \rho_2}\]

Applied twice — first to (canopy + floor), then to (cover + canopy-floor) — this gives the effective fraction of the incident NIR that ends up at each internal surface:

\[\tau_{can,NIR} = e^{-K_{NIR} \cdot LAI}, \quad \rho_{can,NIR} = \rho_{can,NIR}^{0} \cdot (1 - e^{-K_{NIR} \cdot LAI})\]

\[(\tau_{CF}, \rho_{CF}) = \mathrm{MultiLayer}(\tau_{can,NIR}, 1 - \rho_{flr,NIR}, \rho_{can,NIR}, \rho_{flr,NIR})\]

\[(\tau_{CCF}, \rho_{CCF}) = \mathrm{MultiLayer}(\tau_{cov,NIR}, \tau_{CF}, \rho_{cov,NIR}, \rho_{CF})\]

\[\alpha_{flr,NIR}^{eff} = \tau_{CCF}, \qquad \alpha_{can,NIR}^{eff} = 1 - \tau_{CCF} - \rho_{CCF}\]

This is the key correction over a single-pass model: with dark concrete ($\rho_{flr,NIR} = 0.07$) and $LAI = 0$, the effective floor absorption is $\tau_{CCF} \approx 0.82$, not $0.5$.

PAR absorption (canopy and floor)

PAR uses a Beer-Lambert intercept on the canopy plus a floor-reflected term that contributes back to the canopy:

\[R_{can,PAR} = R_{t,PAR} \cdot (1 - \rho_{can,PAR}) \cdot (1 - e^{-K_1 \cdot LAI})\]

\[R_{can,PAR}^{flr} = R_{t,PAR} \cdot e^{-K_1 \cdot LAI} \cdot \rho_{flr,PAR} \cdot (1 - \rho_{can,PAR}) \cdot (1 - e^{-K_2 \cdot LAI})\]

\[R_{flr,PAR} = R_{t,PAR} \cdot e^{-K_1 \cdot LAI} \cdot (1 - \rho_{flr,PAR})\]

with $R_{t,PAR} = I_{glob} \cdot \eta_{PAR} \cdot \tau_{cov,PAR} \cdot (1 - \eta_{glob,air})$.

Default optical properties

ParameterValueDescription
$K_{1,PAR}$0.7First-pass canopy PAR extinction
$K_{2,PAR}$0.7Reflected-PAR canopy extinction
$K_{NIR}$0.27Canopy NIR extinction
$\rho_{can,PAR}$0.07Canopy PAR reflectance
$\rho_{can,NIR}$0.35Canopy NIR reflectance
$\rho_{flr,PAR}$0.07Concrete PAR reflectance
$\rho_{flr,NIR}$0.07Concrete NIR reflectance
$\eta_{glob,air}$0.10Construction obstacle absorption

These match the Modelica defaults; override them by extending the constructor if needed.

Air absorption

Some of the transmitted radiation is absorbed by the construction elements (trusses, gutters, dust on the cover, internal pipes, supports) and released to the air. The Modelica formula treats this as a flat 10 % multiplier:

\[R_{sun \to air} = \eta_{glob,air} \cdot I_{eff} \cdot \left[ \tau_{cov,PAR} \eta_{PAR} + (\alpha_{can,NIR} + \alpha_{flr,NIR}) \eta_{NIR} \right]\]

This term is added directly to the air energy balance ($Q_{sol,air}$), not to the cover or floor — without it the energy budget loses the $\eta_{glob,air}$ fraction silently.

Energy budget

For $I_{glob} = 1\,000$ W/m² with no canopy and dark concrete floor:

TermW/m²%
$R_{sun \to cov}$303.0 %
$R_{sun \to flr}$73973.9 %
$R_{sun \to can}$141.4 %
$R_{sun \to air}$878.7 %
Reflected outside13013.0 %
Total absorbed inside87087 %

The 13 % loss is the reflected light that escapes back through the (clear) cover before being absorbed.

Wall solar gain

When the greenhouse has glazed walls (A_walls > 0), the wall transmission of direct + diffuse beams is computed at every time step using the wall_solar_gain helper:

\[Q_{walls}^{dir} = I_{dir,n} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{4} \max(0, \cos\theta_i) \cdot A_{wall,i}\]

\[Q_{walls}^{diff} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{diff,h} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{4} A_{wall,i}\]

\[R_{walls} = \frac{(Q_{walls}^{dir} + Q_{walls}^{diff}) \cdot \tau_{cov} \cdot (1 - \eta_{glob,air})}{A_{floor}}\]

with $\cos\theta_i = \cos(\text{elev}) \cdot \cos(\text{sun\_az} - \text{wall\_az}_i)$, where the four wall outward normals follow the compass convention used by solar_position (0 = north, 180 = south).

The transmitted flux is added to the floor solar input with the same multi-bounce absorption fraction ($0.93$) as the rest of the cover-incoming radiation. This is what makes the small-greenhouse comparison agree across sizes (a 5 × 5 m greenhouse has 2 m² of wall per m² of floor; the wall contribution dominates).

Direct / diffuse split

interp_solar(tmy, t) returns the instantaneous direct-beam-normal and diffuse-horizontal irradiances. If the EPW provides them (I_dir_n and I_diff_h columns) they are linearly interpolated; otherwise the global horizontal is split with the Erbs (1982) clearness-index correlation:

\[k_t = \frac{I_{glob}}{1367 \cdot \sin(\text{elev})}\]

\[\frac{I_{diff}}{I_{glob}} = \begin{cases} 1 - 0.09 k_t & k_t \le 0.22 \\ 0.9511 - 0.1604 k_t + 4.388 k_t^2 - 16.638 k_t^3 + 12.336 k_t^4 & 0.22 < k_t \le 0.80 \\ 0.165 & k_t > 0.80 \end{cases}\]

with the solar elevation from solar_position (Spencer 1971, evaluated at the TMY's latitude and start_doy).

PV panel effect on solar radiation

When a PV panel is mounted on the roof, two independent parameters control how it modifies the irradiance reaching the greenhouse:

  • Coverage $c$: fraction of roof area covered (0–1)
  • Transmittance $\tau_{PV}$: fraction of light passing through each panel (0 for opaque Si, 0.4 for thin-film, 0.65 for BIPV glass)

The effective irradiance entering the greenhouse is:

\[I_{eff} = \left[(1 - c) + c \cdot \tau_{PV}\right] \times I_{glob}\]

The absorbed fraction generates heat and electricity:

\[P_{elec} = c \cdot \alpha_{PV} \cdot \eta(T_{cell}) \cdot I_{glob}\]

\[Q_{PV,heat} = c \cdot \alpha_{PV} \cdot I_{glob} - P_{elec}\]

Cell temperature (Faiman 2008)

\[T_{cell} = T_{out} + \frac{I_{glob}}{U_0 + U_1 \cdot u_{wind}}\]

with $U_0 = 25$ W/(m²·K) and $U_1 = 6.84$ W·s/(m³·K).

Temperature coefficient

\[\eta(T) = \eta_{STC} \times [1 + \beta \times (T_{cell} - 25\,°C)]\]

Technology$\beta$Typical $\eta_{STC}$
Crystalline Si (opaque)−0.40 %/°C18–22 %
Thin-film / OPV (semi-transparent)−0.25 %/°C8–12 %

Current status and remaining limitations

Implemented:

  • Multi-layer NIR distribution (canopy + floor + cover) — matches the original Modelica MultiLayer_TauRho exactly.
  • Air absorption term ($R_{sun \to air}$) routed to the air energy balance.
  • PAR floor reflectance contributing back to canopy.
  • Wall solar gain on 4 cardinal façades, with direct/diffuse split via Erbs (1982) when EPW data is missing.
  • Geometric solar position (Spencer 1971), exposed via interp_solar(tmy, t).
  • Canopy energy balance node ($T_{canopy}$) coupled to the air node via sensible heat, latent heat (Stanghellini transpiration), and IR radiation to the cover.
  • Dynamic stomatal conductance (Vialet-Chabrand 2017) as optional state variable.
  • PV panel transmittance, waste heat, and temperature-corrected efficiency (Faiman 2008).
  • VPL geometry transfer for 3D Monte Carlo ray-tracing comparison.

Remaining limitations:

  • Wall solar gain assumes 4 cardinal walls of equal area; for non-rectangular buildings the user must aggregate the geometry into an equivalent square footprint.
  • The cover-incoming radiation is treated as if it falls on a horizontal surface (Beer-Lambert with $K_{NIR} = 0.27$); no angular correction for the cosine of the solar zenith — fine for a flat roof, slightly off for steeply sloped Venlo geometries.
  • No spectral model beyond PAR/NIR.
